coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis and characterization of highly luminescent nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots and their application as highly selective and sensitive nanoprobes for low level detection of uranyl ion in hair and water samples and application to cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Molaei, Karam
Molaabasi, Fatemeh
Hosseinkhani, Saman
Alizadeh, Naader
Alipour, Mohsen
Moassess, Soroush
coredata.dc:description: Innovative water-soluble nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) with bright luminescence and high quantum yields of 35.0% were synthesized via a simple and straightforward hydrothermal method, using an aqueous solution of alendronate sodium as a novel N and P containing single-source precursor. UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopies, TEM, AFM, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering and FT-IR spectroscopy were used to characterize the morphological, structural and optical properties of the synthesized N,P-CDs. The N,P-CDs with excellent water solubility and biocompatibility possess a strong greenish blue emission centered at 420nm (λem =330nm). Due to the existence and special response of bisphosphonate groups to uranyl ions (UO2 2+), the fluorescence signal of N,P-CDs found to rapidly and selectively quench by this important cation. The linear response range to UO2 2+ was from 0.01 to 2.5μM with a limit of detection of 4.5nM (S/N=3). The prepared N,P-CDs was then applied to highly sensitive and selective detection of traces of uranyl ion in water and hair samples. Moreover, the prepared N,P-CDs were used as a nice bioimaging agent in BT474 cells, so that the cytotoxicity and cellular uptake of N,P-CDs were investigated by a MTT assay and through a localization study, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N- and P-doped carbon dots
Alendronate sodium
Fluorescence
Uranyl ion detection
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of the N,P-CDs to uranyl ions
Selectivity of N,P-CDs as a nanoprobe for detection of metal ions
Detection of uranyl ions in water and hair samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N,P-CDs. The inset shows size-distribution of the N,P-CDs obtained from the TEM. (b) AFM image of the N,P-CDs and its thickness profile along the line. The height profiles of the N,P-CDs obtained from AFM image. (c) DLS size dispersion and (d) FT-IR spectrum of N,P-CDs. The TEM and AFM samples were prepared by dropping a 25-times dilution of the as-synthesized N,P-CDs solution onto carbon-coated copper grid and mica surface, followed by the solvent evaporation.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum of the N,P-CDs. (b) High-resolution C 1s, (c) high-resolution O 1s, (d) high-resolution N 1s, and (e) high-resolution P 2p XPS spectra of the N,P-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation (λem =420nm), and fluorescence (λex =350nm) emission spectra of N,P-CDs. Inset shows optical photographs of the as-synthesized N,P-CDs in water under a visible light (left) and under a UV beam of 365nm, displaying a bright blue photoluminescence (right). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra for different excitation wavelengths from 300nm to 410nm. Inset shows the normalized fluorescence emissions spectra recorded from 300 to 420nm in 10nm increments. (c) Relative FL intensities of the N,P-CDs in different organic solvents. (d) Comparison of fluorescence emission spectra of the N,PCDs and quinine sulfate. The used concentration of N,P-CDs was 0.01mgmL−1 in (a–c). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of N,P-CDs from alendronate sodium by hydrothermal route and the highly selective detection of uranyl.

(a) FL spectra of the N,P-CDs probes (2μgmL−1) 2min after addition of various concentration of uranyl ion (from top to bottom: 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 10.0, 30.0, 50.0, and 100μM). Inset shows the photographs of N,P-CDs aqueous solutions after addition of different concentrations of uranyl ions (i.e., 0.0, 0.1, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0μM) under a 365nm UV light. (b) Stern−Volmer plot for fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs by uranyl ions (c). FL spectra of N,P-CDs treated with different metal ions (50μM, except for uranyl with concentration of 2.5μM). (d) Selectivity of N,P-CDs nanoprobe for uranyl ion over other metal ions. The concentration of uranyl ion and other metal ions were 2.5μM and 50μM, respectively (blue 2.5μM)). F0 and F are N,P-CDs fluorescence intensities at 420nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence decay profile of N,P-CDs in absence and presence of uranyl ion. (b) Absorption spectra of N,P-CDs, uranyl and N,P-CDs/uranyl solution. (c) Cell viability of BT-474 cells mixed with different concentration of N, P-CDs for 24h, 48h and 72h at 37°C. (d) fluorescence images of BT474 cells treated with N,P-CDs (0.2mgmL−1) under excitation wavelength of 405nm (d2) and 488nm (d4), bright field (d1), and (d3) and (d5) the merged images of (d2) and (d4), respectively.
Limit of detection (LOD) and response time comparison of some uranyl ion detection methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Silver decorated reduced graphene oxide sheets.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Dependent on the oligonucleotide probe length.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of recovery studies of uranyl ions from tap, river, mineral, and hair samples spiked with by N,P-CDs and ICP-EOS.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis and characterization of highly luminescent nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots and their application as highly selective and sensitive nanoprobes for low level detection of uranyl ion in hair and water samples and application to cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Innovative water-soluble nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) with bright luminescence and high quantum yields of 35.0% were synthesized via a simple and straightforward hydrothermal method, using an aqueous solution of alendronate sodium as a novel N and P containing single-source precursor. UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopies, TEM, AFM, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering and FT-IR spectroscopy were used to characterize the morphological, structural and optical properties of the synthesized N,P-CDs. The N,P-CDs with excellent water solubility and biocompatibility possess a strong greenish blue emission centered at 420nm (λem =330nm). Due to the existence and special response of bisphosphonate groups to uranyl ions (UO2 2+), the fluorescence signal of N,P-CDs found to rapidly and selectively quench by this important cation. The linear response range to UO2 2+ was from 0.01 to 2.5μM with a limit of detection of 4.5nM (S/N=3). The prepared N,P-CDs was then applied to highly sensitive and selective detection of traces of uranyl ion in water and hair samples. Moreover, the prepared N,P-CDs were used as a nice bioimaging agent in BT474 cells, so that the cytotoxicity and cellular uptake of N,P-CDs were investigated by a MTT assay and through a localization study, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs), as emerging light-emitting nanoprobes, have attracted an increasing attention because of their unique photo-luminescence properties, chemical and photo stability, negligible cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility [1–4] As a result, extensive research attempts have been paid to engineering of CDs to tune their intrinsic properties, e.g., electronic characteristics and surface and local chemical features [5–7]. However, there is still a challenge to fabricate highly fluorescent CDs through a facile strategy and effective method. Surface passivation or heteroatom doping into the carbon skeleton was often employed to avoid self-quenching phenomenon resulted in their substantial ensemble Stokes shift and increased quantum yields (QYs) of CDs [5]. It was confirmed that the primary amines and phosphates, which can act as N- and P-doping precursors and surface passivation agents, both considerably enhanced the photoluminescence of CDs, especially P indicates a stronger effect on the polarizability of the fluorescent carbon materials and thus producing a new nanoprobe for a wide range of electrochemical and optical applications, like oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), photocatalysis, drug delivery, biomedicine, biological imaging, and sensing [8] With selection of a proper precursor and a single starting material, the formation, doping and surface functionalizing of CDs would be carried out simultaneously. With this approach, the heteroatoms not only enhance the quantum yields of CDs, but also causing functionalization of the surface of CDs that facilitate them to interact selectively with special analytes.

Uranium, a heavy metal whose chemical toxicity is poorly understood, is the main fuel in nuclear industries [9]. Nuclear activities, application of high density projectiles against armored vehicles and soil fertilization by uranium released from phosphate fertilizers have all resulted in an increased level of uranium-release into environment [10]. The exposure to abnormal and even sub-microgram concentrations of uranium may have huge impact on cardiovascular, reproductive and immune system within a short time span [11]. In the past few decades, nuclear pollution has gained global environmental concerns with the rapid development of nuclear industry and the peaceful utilization of nuclear energy. World Health Organization (WHO) recommended the maximum allowable uranium concentration in drinking water to be less than 20μgL−1 [12,13].

Uranyl ion (UO2 2+) is the most stable specie of uranium, which existing in the minerals, seawater and mammalian body [14] Due to high toxicity and radioactivity of uranyl ions, its measurement is of utmost importance in environmental samples, to aim people and environment protection from uranium damages. Up to now, different analytical methods have been used to determine uranyl ions concentration, including spectrophotometric methods [15], X-ray fluorescence technique (XRF) [16] inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [12,17], ion imprinted polymer (IIP) separation and inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) detection [18–20] photoacoustic imaging [21], quantum dots fluorescence [22], surface enhanced Raman scattering [23], potentiometric ion-selective electrodes [24,25], PVC-membrane optical sensors [26], and fluorescence optode membranes [27]. Although, ICP-MS and XRF are the most proper techniques for routine analysis owing to their better sensitivities, total operation and equipment cost of these methods is high, which caused them not be accessible as a routine instrument in the most of laboratories [12]. Among various methods, fluorescent sensors are particularly attractive because of their high sensitivity, and no need for expensive sophisticated instrumentation, toxic regent and solvent, and sample preparation prior to analysis.

In 2006, Wang et al. reported the modification of magnetic nanoparticles with bisphosphonate ligand for the removal of uranyl ions from water and blood samples [28]. Although a high amount of phosphate and proteins exist in real samples, which can interact to uranyl ions competitively, the high affinity of bisphosphonate ligands for uranyl ions allows for selective binding via chelating effect. Meanwhile, some attempts have been devoted to the electrodes chemically modified with phosphate/phosphine derivatives terminal groups for on-site rapid and selective detection of uranyl ion [29]. Besides, it has been reported that the electron transfer process in phosphorus co-doped nitrogen containing carbon dots is superior to that in boron co-doped nitrogen containing carbon dots, most probably due to excess electron incorporation [7]. These works inspired us to search for a specific molecule with both primary amine and bisphosphonate moieties in its structure for the preparation of new uranyl-selective CDs.

Thus, in this work, we used alendronate sodium as a suitable single carbon precursor containing N functional groups (primary amines) and bisphosphonate moiety, in order to synthesis N,P-doped CDs. The former characteristic led to the high quantum yield of CDs and the latter resulted in functionalization of the surface of CDs and, consequently, in enhancement of binding selectivity. It should be noted that alendronate sodium is a bone re-sorption inhibitor which is commonly used in prevention and treatment of bone diseases including Paget’s disease, osteoporesis, and metastatic bone disease [30]. To the best of our knowledge, the determination of uranyl ion with applying CDs as a sensitive fluorescence nanoprobe has not been reported in the literature. Thus, by taking alendronate sodium as the starting precursor followed by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, without using any organic solvents or additives, we were able to prepare nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs (N,P-CDs) that can act as a multifunctional nanoplatform with a bright greenish blue photoluminescence and high QY (∼35.0%). Furthermore, thanks to the intrinsic advantages of these novel CDs, such as easy preparation, favorable biocompatibility and photostability, low toxicity, and unique combination of functionality and carbon materials, we thoroughly investigated both the feasibility of the N,P-CDs as a selective probe for low level detection of uranyl ion, via fluorescence quenching, and also their suitability for cell bioimaging processes.

Pure alendronate sodium was purchased as Fosamax and used without further purification. UO2(NO3)2·6H2O (depleted uranium, 99%) was purchased from Fluka. All other reagents were of analytical grade from Merck and Fluka and used as received. The double deionized water used throughout the study was provided using a Milli-Q system from Millipore (Bedford, MA, USA).

Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Perkin-Elmer LS–50B fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp. The spectral band pass was set at 10nm for both excitation and emission wavelengths. The absorbance spectra were recorded on a Model Scinco UV S-2100 using a 1.0cm quartz cuvette over the wavelength range of 250–800nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer with the KBr pellet technique. The size of the N,P-CDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a Zeiss transmission electron microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 150kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) experiments were performed under ambient conditions using a Veeco CP Research instrument using a Si cantilever in the contact mode. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were carried out using an ESCALab220I-XL spectrometer (VG company, U.K.), and an Al Kα X-ray was applied as the excitation source (hν =1486.6eV) for the XPS measurements. Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were measured using a Zetasizer nano ZS series dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, U.K.).

N,P-CDs were synthesized via a simple and straightforward hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment, 300mg of alendronate sodium as nitrogen, phosphor and carbon source was thoroughly dissolved in 100mL of double deionized water. There was no detectable fluorescence signal from the solution. The resultant solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 20h. Upon completion of hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the yellow colored solution was collected. The reaction product was centrifuged at 10000rpm for 10min to remove the larger particles and precipitated black particles. After that, the yellow supernatant was dialyzed over DI water in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 3500Da) to remove small molecules for three days and stored in refrigerator for fluorescence studies. In order to acquire a powder of the N,P-CDs for FT-IR and XPS analysis, a portion of dialyzed sample was freeze-dried. QY of the as-synthesized N,P-CDs was determined using optically matching solutions of quinine sulfate (QY=0.546 in 1M H2SO4) as a standard at an excitation wavelength of 330nm. QY value was calculated by a widely accepted relative method using the following equation:
Q
Y x
= Q
Y R
×
A R
A X
×
I x
I R
×
η
x 2
η
R 2
where R and X refer to the standard and the sample of interest, respectively, I is the integrated emission spectra, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the absorbance. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorption was kept below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength (330nm).

Firstly, 3μL of the as-prepared N, P-CDs was added into 997μL phosphate buffer solution (20mM, pH 7.4) and mixed thoroughly at room temperature. Subsequently, the resulting solution was titrated by successive addition of various concentrations of uranyl ion (0–50μM) and fluorescence spectra of the mixture solutions were recorded after 1min. All the fluorescence assays were performed at an excitation wavelength of 330nm recording the emission between 340 and 700nm, at room temperature. Based on these measurements, the linear Stern-Volmer equation of F0/F=1+KSV [Q], where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in absence and presence of the uranyl ion, was established to represent the photoluminescence quenching. Here, KSV is the Stern-Volmer PL quenching constant, which indicates to the sensitivity of the sensing platform to the quencher (Q). To investigate the selectivity of detecting uranyl ions, fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs treated with 2.5μM uranyl ions in presence of different cations (50μM) was separately recorded. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

Mineral, tap and river water were collected from local supermarket, Tehran city tap water and Seimare River (Ilam, Iran), respectively, filtered immediately through 0.45μm membrane filters and stored in a refrigerator prior to analyses. Scalp hair samples were taken from two adult man individuals, living in Tehran province (Iran), who visited a barbershop for haircuts. In order to omit lipids and environmental pollutants hair samples were firstly washed with shampoo and acetone. Then, the hair samples were digested as follow, 1.5g of hair sample was carefully weighed, then added into a beaker containing of 10mL of HNO3 and 3mL of H2O2. Next, the solution was heated on a hot plate at 100°C for 1h and allowed to evaporate to near dryness. After that, the digested sample was diluted by double distilled water up to 1mL and was centrifuged for 10min at 7000. The analytical procedure for detection of uranyl ion in digested samples was the similar to that above. Recovery tests were also performed to evaluate the effect of interferences and the accuracy of the method. To this aim, uranyl ion was added to the blank hair in final concentrations of 5 and 50μgg−1.

The cytotoxicity study of the N, P-CDs ware performed via a standard MTT test on BT474 cells. In brief, BT474 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (2×104 cells per well) and different concentrations of the N,P-CDs (i.e, 0.00, 0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50, 1.00mgmL−1) were added to the cells and incubated for 24, 48 and 72h at 37°C in humidified incubator with 5% CO2 atmosphere. 10μL of 5mgmL−1 of the MTT salt solution was added to each well and incubated for 4h. The formazan crystals formed were dissolved in DMSO (100μL) and the plates were read in a microplate reader (ELx800, Biotek, USA) at 570nm.

BT474 cells were first grown with RPMI medium in 24 well plats for 10h in a humid atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37°C. Subsequently, a solution of N,P-CDs (ca. μL; 10mgmL−1) at pH 7.14 was added to the medium culture of the cells and incubated for different time intervals. To remove the excess N,P-CDs, the culture was washed several times with PBS buffer (20m, pH 7.4). Finally, cell imaging was recorded by using a 3 Cell Cytation™ Imaging Multi-Mode Reader (BioTek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA).

The morphology and size of the as-prepared N,P-CDs were verified by TEM, AFM and DLS measurements. As can be seen from Fig. 1 a, the N,P-CDs are well dispersed with a quasi-spherical morphology by an average size of about 5nm from 100 counts (inset of Fig. 1a). The corresponding AFM image in tapping mode shown in Fig. 1b also indicates that the N,P-CDs are in the order of 5nm in diameter and quasi-spherical shape. The height profile in Fig. 1c reveals that the mean height of the N,P-CDs is about 2nm. In addition, the size distribution characterized by DLS (Fig. 1d) ascertains a mean hydrodynamic diameter of nearly 8nm, which is in consistence with the TEM and AFM results, considering the fact that DLS estimates the whole hydrodynamic size of particles. The zeta potential measurement proved that the surface of the N,P-CDs is highly negatively charged, with a zeta potential of −22.4mV (Fig. S1, in SI), suggesting that most of the N,P-CDs are well soluble and stable in water solutions owing to the existence of hydrophilic carboxylic, phosphonate and hydroxyl moieties at the surface, which will be proved by FT-IR and XPS measurements. Besides, these moieties bring a series of emissive traps on the surface, which lead to the strong photoluminescent property of the CDs [31,32].

FT-IR spectroscopy was applied to characterize the surface functional groups on N,P-CDs, as shown in corresponding spectrum (Fig. 1d). The broad and strong peak between 3208.9 and 3563.8cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups [33,34]. The characteristic peaks at 1257.4, 2645.8, 695.2 and 1025.9cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of PO, POH, PC and POR respectively [35], indicating that the phosphonate groups are successfully transferred from alendronate molecules to the N,P-CDs surface during CDs formation. In addition, the peak located at 1130.1cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of hydrogen-bonded PO groups from phosphates or polyphosphates [6]. Also, the strong peaks located at 2923.5 and 2854.1cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CH, confirming the abundance of nongraphitizing chains on the N,P-CDs surface. The emergence of new peaks at 1442.3, 1735.6, and 1025.9cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of the N,P-CDs in comparison with alendronate sodium (see Fig. S2) can be assigned to the characteristic stretching vibrations for CC in aromatic hydrocarbons (proving the existence an aromatic skeleton in N,P-CDs), stretching vibration of CO groups, and vibration of POR (R=aromatic structure), respectively [36,37]. These characteristic peaks confirm the successful preparation of the N,P-CDs.
(Fig. 1)

Next, the XPS analysis was performed on the N,P-CDs to investigate the elemental analysis and chemical bonds. The full survey spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2 a, clearly indicates that the surface of N,P-CDs is mainly composed of four major elements including carbon, oxygen, phosphorus and nitrogen. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) reveals four peaks located at 284.5, 285.3, 286.0, and 286.7eV, which are assigned to the C−C/CC groups (sp2C) in the aromatic rings, CP, CN/CO, and CO groups, respectively [38,39]. The O 1s spectrum of the N,P-CDs (Fig. 2c) illustrates five peaks centered at 529.2, 529.9, 531.1, 531.8, and 534.3eV corresponding to the CO, PO, COC, COH, and PO groups, respectively [40]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of N, P-CDs (Fig. 1d), depicts the characteristic peaks of CNC (397.6eV), pyridine-like N (398.7eV), pyrrole-like N (400.7eV), and amino N (399.6eV), supporting the fact that the nitrogen-containing groups are doped on the P-CDs [41,42]. The de-convoluted P 2p spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows two peaks at 132.4 and 133.7eV corresponding to the C-P and P-O groups, respectively [15,23]. The XPS studies are thus consistent with our FT-IR investigation, discussed abbove. Based on these results, we can conclude that the binding between N,P-CDs and uranyl ions is most likely be owing to the complexation of UO2 2+ with bisphosphonate groups of N,P-CDs [16].

The optical properties of the N,P-CDs including UV−Vis absorption, excitation fluorescence emission, photostability and QY were subsequently explored. The N,P-CDs, synthesized via a single step hydrothermal synthetic route from a single starting material alendronate sodium, the color of the as-prepared solution of N, P-CDs was greenish yellow (inset of Fig. 3 a, left panel) and showed a strong greenish blue photoluminescence when illuminating under UV lamp (inset of Fig. 3a, right panel), indicating the formation of luminescent carbon nanoparticles [36]. In UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3a), the N,P-CDs display two typical absorption peaks, a sharp on around 230 and a broad spectrum at 240–340nm. The peak located at 230nm is assigned to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains [38]. The prominent broad absorption peak attributes likely to the formation of excited defect surface states induced by the P and N heteroatoms and the n→π* transition of CO [43,44].

Upon excitation at 330nm (the optimal excitation wavelength), the N,P-CDs exhibited a maximum emission at λem =420nm (Fig. 3a). Similar to the other luminescent carbon nanoparticles, the prepared N,P-CDs also depicted an excitation-dependent photoluminesce behavior, which is originated from different surface states and sizes affecting the band gap of N,P-CDs (Fig. 3b) [45]. The time-resolved fluoresce decay was also performed. The mean lifetime is calculated to be 0.20ns. Such a short time indicates the luminescent mechanism is probably the radioactive recombination of excitations which is appropriate for optoelectronic and bio-applications [19,22]. Further, the PL intensity of the prepared N,P-CDs at the maximum emission (420nm) was monitored to test photostability under continuous illumination at 330nm and long-term storage stability under ambient conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S3, after 70min of continuous illumination and 30days at ambient conditions, the N,P-CDs exhibited no significant reduction in PL intensity. This remarkable resistance to photobleaching displayed by the N,P-CDs could be promising for bio-imaging and bio-labeling applications where the commonly used organic fluorophores suffer from poor photostability [46]. Moreover, the prepared N,P-CDs showed a relatively constant fluorescence emission at broad ranges of ionic strength (up to 1M NaCl), pH (in the range 3–11) and temperature (in the range 5–60°C), which makes the N,P-CDs promising probes for biological applications (Fig. S4).

In addition, the prepared N,P-CDs could remain stable in many of solvents with different polarities (see Fig. S5). Meanwhile, as it is seen from Fig. 3c, the FL intensities of the N,P-CDs increases with increasing solvent polarities, indicating that the existence of polar groups on the surface of N,P-CDs led to different dispersabilities in polar solvents and, consequently, to different FL intensities [47]. The QY of N,P-CDs was estimated using quinine sulfate as reference and found to be 35.3%, as depicted in Fig. 3d. This QY value found to be notably higher than that of many reported luminescent CDs [33–35,38,48–51], which can be originated from the presence of the P and N-containing groups. This might be due to the fact that P and N-containing groups can passivate the surface energy-trapping sites through stabilization of the excitons, thereby improving the photoluminesces properties [21].

Finally, to synthesis of N,P-CDs with highest FL intensity, a convenient temperature and time should be applied to optimize different reaction steps like hydrolysis, dehydration, decomposition, nucleation and growth. Thus, in order to synthesis N,P-CDs with maximum FL intensity by the hydrothermal method, we investigated the effect of different parameters such as concentration of alendronate sodium, reaction temperature and time of hydrothermal treatment and the results were presented in Fig. S6. As is quite obvious, when the aqueous solution containing of 3mgmL−1 of alendronate sodium was allowed to react under at 180°C for 20h, a yellow solution was obtained that showed maximum fluorescence with respect to the other considered conditions. In the other hand, the decrease in FL intensity of the N,P-CDs with an increase in the reaction time (more than 20h) may be attributed to the effect of size change during the nanocrystal growth. This reasoning was made based on our experimental observation of black particles in the medium at the end of the reaction, and that reported in the literature [52]. Therefore, 3mgmL−1 of alendronate sodium, 180°C and 20h were selected as the optimal conditions for the preparation of N,P-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the N,P-CDs to uranyl ions
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of many functional groups especially bisphosphonate groups in the N,P-CDs surface (as demonstrated by FT-IR and XPS measurements) and the superior optical properties aroused us to further explore their potential sensing applications. Due to the special response of bisphosphonate groups to uranyl ions [16,53], the N,P-CDs were directly evaluated as fluorescence nanprobes without any modification for direct determination of uranyl ions. Interestingly, it was observed that the biocompatible N,P-CDs are sensitive toward uranyl ions. As depicted in a suggested sensing process (Fig. 4 ), the observed fluorescence quenching by uranyl ions might be attributed to the strong interaction between uranyl ion and the N,P-CDs, which probably as a result of the selective nonradiative complex formation, which facilitates the transfer of charge from the excited state of the N,P-CDs to uranyl ions that can be catalyzed by the heteroatoms present.

The time-dependent fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs after the addition of uranyl ions was evaluated to obtain the proper detection time for the following experiments. Fig. S8 illustrates that the fluorescence intensity varied swiftly after 2min and no degradation in fluorescence was observed when the incubation time was prolonged to 10min, implying a promising application in the fast sensing of uranyl. A calibration experiment with different concentrations of uranyl ion from 0 to 100μM was performed to evaluate the range of linear detection. From Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CDs was steadily decreased after the addition of uranyl ion, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs was predominantly dependent on uranyl ion concentration. The quenching of N,P-CDs fluorescence intensity was found to be linear from 0.01 to 2.5μM (Fig. 5b), which could be described by ta Stern-Volmer equation with a satisfactory correlation coefficient of 0.992. The slope of the equation was 6.8 105 M−1, which represents the Stern−Volmer constant (KSV). Moreover, the intense greenish blue emission of N,P-CDs permited to observe a gradual quenching process with increasing of uranyl ions when illuminated by photons of λem =365nm in a UV chamber. As it is obvious from inset of Fig. 5a, the addition of increased concentration of uranyl into the N,P-CDs solution led to a clearly recognizable color against the initial solution by the naked eye. Meanwhile, the limit of detection (LOD) for uranyl ion was estimated to be 4.5nM at an S/N ratio of 3. The estimated LOD is below the maximum concentration levels in drinking water authorized by the regulatory agencies (55.5–111.2nM) [13]. The repeatability of the analytical response, expressed as the relative standard deviation (RSD), for five replicate determinations of uranyl ion at a concentration of 0.1μM was estimated as 3.6%.

A comparison between the LOD of the proposed method with that reported for several previously reported techniques for the detection of uranyl ion is depicted in Table 1
[10,54–58]. Our sensing approach provided a LOD value that is comparable to those acquired by using complicate and expensive instruments (e.g., ICP-MS). It is worth mentioning that the LOD of our “turn-ff” system was 4.5nM, which shows a significant improvement over the LOD value of 82.8μM obtained through a “turn-on” fluorescence method developed by Chen et al. [14]. It is also noteworthy that our sensing approach does not require a long response time and any modification and pre-concentration step. In addition, the CDs are less expensive than reagents such as DNAzymes [54,56] and exhibited significantly lower levels of toxicity than CdS QDs [55]. Short analysis time of our proposed method provided the possibility of its application in standard analytical laboratories. Therefore, our proposed sensor, although working in a “turn-off” mode, offers a high sensitive and selective response towards uranyl ions caused by well-engineered surface functionality of the N,P-CDs via selecting a suitable precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N,P-CDs as a nanoprobe for detection of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the interfering effect of the potentially interfering ions on the detection ability of N,P-CDs for uranyl ions, the fluorescence response of N,P-CDs to uranyl was examined in the presence of excess amounts of other cations. As expected, the N,P-CDs displayed a high selectivity for uranyl over other metal ions tested. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of the N,P-CDs (2μgmL−1) solution was clearly quenched by uranyl at the concentration of 2.5μM. On the contrary, most of the other tested ions (i.e., Ag+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cr3+) cannot induce noticeable quenching effects on N,P-CDs fluorescence intensity at a 20-folds higher concentration, while Cu2+ and Hg2+ exhibited relatively weak quenching. This difference can be attributed to the presence of bisphosphonate groups on the N,P-CDs surface, which have high binding affinity for uranyl [7], thereby facilitating the charge/electron transfer, resulting fluorescence quenching of the N,P-CDs.

To further confirm that the fluorescence quenching is related to the complex formation between bisphosphonate groups on the N,P-CDs surface with uranyl ions, a control test was performed using another type of CDs obtained from glucose [51]. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the glucose based CDs, without bisphosphonate groups, was not quenched in the presence of uranyl ion, even at 50μM concentration (Fig. S8), indicating that the formation of nonradiative complex plays a key role in the selective fluorescence response of the N,P-CDs towards the uranyl ions. Accordingly, the N,P-CDs could be used as specific fluorescent nanoprobes for uranyl detection.

Fluorescence quenching of a fluorophore occurs through different processes including energy transfer, complex formation, excited state reaction and collisional quenching. Several experiments were performed to elucidate the PL quenching mechanism of N,P-CDs towards uranyl ion. The fluorescence decays of the N,P-CDs at the emission maximum were first recorded in the absence and presence of uranyl and results are shown in Fig. 6 a. The decay time of N,P-CDs before and after addition of uranyl ion (N,P-CDs/uranyl) was 0.20 and 0.17ns, respectively. The change in the fluorescence decay time was no significant, ruling out any dynamic quenching processes [31]. Moreover, the bimolecular quenching constant (kq) of the N,P-CDs was found to be 3.4×1015 M−1 s−1 using the equation kq=Ksv/τ0, where τ0 is the lifetime of N,P-CDs in the absence of quencher, while KSV is 6.8×105 M−1, evaluated from the slope of the linear Stern-Volmer plot. Considering the fact that maximum value of kq for a diffusion controlled quenching process is about 1010 M−1 s−1 [59], the obtained large value of 3.4×1015 M−1 s−1 for kq suggested the occurrence of a specific interaction between N,P-CDs and uranyl ions resulting in non-luminescent complex formation and, hence, the fluorescence quenching of N,P-CDs. These results indicated that the dominating quenching mechanism is a simple static quenching caused by coordination through bisphosphonate and amino groups on the N,P-CDs surfaces, acting as an electron donor or/and acceptor to change the surface states of N,P-CDs, however, the chelating effect induces more coordination between uranyl and bisphosphonate [28,39,59]. As can be seen in Fig. 6b, the addition of uranyl ion led to the change in the absorption spectrum of N,P-CDs, indicating the formation of N,P-CDs-uranyl complex, supporting the notion of static quenching process, too.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of uranyl ions in water and hair samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to elucidate applicability of the fluorescent nanoprobes in sensing of uranyl ion in real samples, the N,P-CDs were used for detection of uranyl in water and scalp hair samples by the standard addition method. In addition to these, some recovery studies were accomplished by spiking the samples containing various uranyl concentrations. It was found that no detectable uranyl existing in all four original samples, which was consistent with the result obtained from the ICP-OES (Vista-Pro, Varian, USA) method. Meanwhile, as can be seen from Table 2 , the recoveries of uranyl in the water and hair samples changed between 93.3% and 106.6%, showing no matrix interferences in detection of different concentrations of uranyl ion.

The accuracy of the fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of uranyl was explored by analyzing a certificated reference material of hair (Human Hair IAEA-086). After digestion of certified hair sample, 100-fold dilution samples were directly submitted to the fluorescent nanoprobe. The results revealed that the amount of uranyl ion in the hair sample was in satisfactory agreement with the certified value for uranyl; i.e., a significant difference did not observed between the certified and experimental values (p=0.05, in t-test.). These results ascertain the validity of the N,P-CDs fluorescence sensing method for the detection of uranyl in real samples. We believe that the proposed method can be applied to practical detection of uranyl in different samples and assist in the prevention of human diseases and environmental pollution caused by the cation.

Bestowed with superior photoluminescence properties, e.g., high quantum yield, high stability with no photobeleaching, and usually great biocompatibility of carbon dots, the N,P-CDs are suggested to serve as a bio-labeling agent. To reveal such potential bio-applications, the cellular toxicity and imaging assays were preliminarily explored. The cell cytotoxicity experiments of the N,P-CDs were studied using BT-474 cells through the standard MTT assay. It was found that the N,P-CDs show a dose-dependent cytotoxicity. As indicated in Fig. 6c, the N,P-CDs did not show significant cytotoxicity to the cells, even at such a high concentration of 1.0mgmL−1 after 48h, although these particles revealed no apparent cytotoxicity up to 0.5mgmL−1 after 72h. Hence the N,P-CDs seem to be promising agents for in vitro and in vivo imaging and, consequently, can be readily substituted with the conventional organic dyes having photobleaching properties and/or semiconductor QDs with biotoxicity in imaging [4,32,33].

After that, the cellular imaging capability of the N, P-CDs to the living cells is studied. Fig. 6d1, d2 and d4 illustrates fluorescence images of BT474 cells treated with N,P-CDs at bright field and excitation wavelengths of 405 and 488nm, respectively. Fig. 6d3 and 6d5 shows the merged images with the bright field and the fluorescence images. It was found from these images that the emissions were located mostly at the whole cell region, thus indicating that the N,P-CDs not only pass through cell membranes, but also easily enter into the cytoplasm and thus label the cell [31,41,47]. It is worthy noted that the morphologies of BT474 cells nearly remained unchanged after the incubation with N,P-CDs, confirming the minimal toxicity of CDs towards bioimaging [4]. These results indicate clearly that N,P-CDs could be used as a novel nanoprobe which able not only to detect uranyl with high specificity, but also to image the cells as a bio-labeling agent.

In summary, a straightforward and green synthetic approach for the simultaneous synthesis, doping and surface functionalization of the luminescence nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (CDs) has developed by the first use of alendronate sodium as a single starting material and a P and N source. The fluorescence signal of the synthesized N,P-CDs was significantly quenched upon addition of uranyl ions based on high affinity between bisphosphonate and uranyl ions. The sensing system illustrated several advantages, such as water solubility, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity and accuracy, short response time, broad liner range, and low detection limit. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report in which novel N and P doped-CDs allow the successful life-cell labeling and hence can act as nanoprobes for fluorescent sensing and imaging systems with no need for any further chemical modification. Meanwhile, the prepared N,P-CDs demonstrated high photostability, excellent cell-permeability, substantially improved fluorescence QY, excitation-dependent emission, negligible cytotoxicity and stability in water and a number of organic solvents, which making them compatible with the commercially available organic dyes and semiconductor QDs in luminescence brightness and in in-vivo imaging. Furthermore, the luminescence properties and surface functionality of the N,P-CDs could also be engineered by selecting a suitable precursor in the synthesis step to construct new multifunctional optical materials with versatile potential applications in biosensing, drug delivery, tissue engineering, optoelectronics and solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the Tarbiat Modares and Razi Universities Research Councils and Iran Science Elites Federation (ISEF).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.018.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications

Long-wavelength, multicolor, and white-light emitting carbon-based dots: achievements made, challenges remaining, and applications

Removal of uranium(VI) from contaminated sediments by surfactants

Uranium determination using atomic spectrometric techniques: an overview

Trace analysis of uranyl ion (UO2 2+) in aqueous solution by fluorescence turn-on detection via aggregation induced emission enhancement effect

Advances in technologies for the measurement of uranium in diverse matrices
Functionalization of chitosan with 3,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid for the adsorption/collection of uranium in water samples and its determination by inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry

Adsorption of uranyl ion onto an Aanthraquinone based ion-imprinted copolymer
Imprinted polymer grafted from silica particles for on-line trace enrichment and ICP OES determination of uranyl ion

Silver nanoparticle decorated reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheet: a platform for SERS based low-level detection of uranyl ion

Flow injection potentiometry by a novelc graphite electrode based on 5-(9-anthracenylmethyl)-5-aza-2,8-dithia[9],(2,9)-1,10-phenanthrolinophane for the selective determination of uranyl ions

Highly selective and sensitive fluorescence optode membrane for uranyl ion based on 5-(9-anthracenylmethyl)-5-aza-2,8-dithia[9],(2,9)-1,10-phenanthrolinophane
A biocompatible method of decorporation: bisphosphonate-modified magnetite nanoparticles to remove uranyl ions from blood
Electrochemical determination of uranyl ions using a self-assembled monolayer
Spectroscopic and HPLC methods for the determination of alendronate in tablets and urine
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-Imaging agents
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent itrogen-doped amphoteric carbon dots for bioimaging from bombyx mori silk – natural proteins
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dot as a nanocarrier for doxorubicin delivery and biological imaging
Good's buffer derived highly emissive carbon quantum dots: excellent biocompatible anticancer drug carrier
Water-soluble and phosphorus-containing carbon dots with strong green fluorescence for cell labeling
Phosphorus: and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for real-time measurement of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species inside macrophages

One-pot green synthesis of high quantum yield oxygen-doped, nitrogen-rich, photoluminescent polymer carbon nanoribbons as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of silver(I) and mercury(II) ions
Non-redox modulated fluorescence strategy for sensitive and selective ascorbic acid detection with highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles via solid-state synthesis
Bright-yellow-emissive N-doped carbon dots: preparation, cellular imaging, and bifunctional sensing
Simple and green synthesis of nitrogen-Doped photoluminescent carbonaceous nanospheres for bioimaging
Doped carbon nanoparticles as a nnew platform for highly photoluminescent dots
Chemiluminescence of carbon dots under strong alkaline solutions: a novel insight into carbon dot optical properties
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
One step synthesis of C-dots by microwave mediated caramelization of poly(ethylene glycol)
Hair-derived carbon dots toward versatile multidimensional fluorescent materials

A fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based “turn-on” nanofluorescence sensor using a nitrogen-doped carbon dot-hexagonal cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheet architecture and application to α-glucosidase inhibitor screening
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence
Intrinsically fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission derived from hydrothermal treatment of glucose in the presence of monopotassium phosphate
One-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots in noncoordinating solvents
Interactions between uranium(vi) and phosphopeptide: experimental and theoretical investigations
Highly sensitive and selective colorimetric sensors for uranyl (UO2 2+): Development and comparison of labeled and label-free DNAzyme-gold nanoparticle systems
Highly sensitive and selective method for detecting ultratrace levels of aqueous uranyl ions by strongly photoluminescent-responsive amine-modified cadmium sulfide quantum dots
Electrochemical uranyl cation biosensor with DNA oligonucleotides as receptor layer

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer quenching at the surface of graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive detection of TNT

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mojtaba Shamsipur has a PhD in analytical chemistry obtained at MichiganState University in 1979. He is currently a professor of chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran. His research works have been mainly focused on the thermodynamic and kinetic studies of macrocyclic ligand complexes and their analytical applications in areas such as ion-transport through liquid membranes, solid-phase extraction, ion-selective-electrodes and optical sensors. In the past decade he has been involved in the synthesis and characterization of a range of novel nano-platforms including highly fluorescent quantum dots, carbon dots, polymer dots and protein-capped noble metal nanoclusters as well as novel nanomotors and different carbon nanomaterials and their applications in preparation of a number of novel electrochemical and fluorescence chemical sensors, biosensors, immunosensors and aptasensors. He has thus far published over 800 scientific papers in international journals.
Karam Molaei is currently a graduate student in Chemistry at Tarbiat Modares University, working toward his PhD degree under the supervision of Prof. Shamsipur.

Fatemeh Molaabasi has received her PhD in Chemistry from Trabiat Modarres University in 2015 under the supervision of professor Shamsipur. She is currently a postdoctoral member of Shamsipur’s research group working on the synthesis and characterization of different protein capped noble metal nanocusters and their applications to preparation of new fluorescence bio-sensing and bio-imaging systems.

Saman Hosseinkhani has received his PhD in Biochemistry in 2002 from the Institute of Biochemistry & Biophysics (IBB), University of Tehran. He is currently a professor of Biochemistry at Tarbiat Modares University. He has published over 180 scientific papers mainly in Biochemistry, Molecular Biology, Genetics and Medicine.

Naader Alizadeh is currently a professor of analytical chemistry at Tarbiat Modares University with specialty in exchange kinetics and electrochemical studies of micellization. His current research interests cover chemical and electrochemical synthesis of nano-structure conducting polymers and their applications in preparation of potentiometric and optical chemical sensors and in development of microextraction methods. Recently, professor Alizadeh and his research group have prepared selective conducting sorbents for electrochemical drug delivery and electrochemically controlled solid phase microextraction and analysis of some drugs and amino acids in biological samples, using different spectroscopic techniques including UV-vis, Fluorescence, IR, NMR, Ion Mobility Spectrometry and Thermal Lens Spectrometry and electrochemical testing.

Mohsen Alipour has received his Ph.D. degree in Nanobiotechnology at Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, in 2017. He currently serves as an Assistant Professor of Nanomedicine at Jahrom University of Medical Sciences. His research interests include development of smart vehicles for gene/drug delivery, manufacturing of micro/nanosensors and development of nanobio toolbox for cell-death and cancer metastasis.

Soroush Moassess is a PhD holder in Nanobiotechnology from Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, in 2017. Her field of study is focused on simultaneous gene delivery and tracking through preparation of photo-luminescent nanoparticles. Recently, she has prepared nanoparticles based on graphene quantum dots and chimeric peptides and studied nuclear internalization and tracking of pDNA in vitro.
